'''Jonah E. Rockoff''' is an American [[education economics|education economist]] and currently works as Professor of Finance and Economics at the [[Columbia Graduate School of Business]].<ref>[https://www0.gsb.columbia.edu/faculty/jrockoff/index.html Faculty profile of Jonah Rockoff on the website of Columbia University. Retrieved April 13th, 2018.]</ref> Rockoff's research interests include the [[economics of education]] and [[public finance]].<ref>[https://scholar.google.com/citations?user=ADm2OHgAAAAJ&hl=en Google Scholar profile of Jonah Rockoff. Retrieved April 13th, 2018.]</ref> His research on the management of public schools has been awarded the 2016 George S. Eccles Research Award in Finance and Economics by [[Columbia Business School]].<ref>[https://www8.gsb.columbia.edu/newsroom/prizes-awards/eccles-prize/research-award List of prize winners of the George S. Eccles Research Award in Finance and Economics on the website of Columbia University. Retrieved April 13th, 2018.]</ref>

== Biography==

Jonah Rockoff earned a [[Bachelor of Arts|B.A.]] in [[economics]] from [[Amherst College]] in 1997, followed by a [[Ph.D.]] in economics from [[Harvard University]] in 2004. Since his graduation, Rockoff has taught and researched at the [[Columbia Graduate School of Business]], first as assistant professor (2004–07), then as associate professor (2008–16), and since 2016 as full professor. Rockoff is affiliated with the [[National Bureau of Economic Research]],<ref>[https://www.nber.org/people/jonah_rockoff Profile of Jonah Rockoff on the website of the National Bureau of Economic Research. Retrieved April 13th, 2018.]</ref> [[Institute of Education Sciences]], [[National Science Foundation]], and the [[Smith Richardson Foundation]]. Moreover, he works as co-editor of the ''[[Journal of Public Economics]]''.<ref>[https://www.journals.elsevier.com/journal-of-public-economics/editorial-board Editorial Board of the Journal of Public Economics. Retrieved April 13th, 2018.]</ref><ref>[https://www0.gsb.columbia.edu/mygsb/faculty/research/pubfiles/3835/cv_jonah_rockoff_current.pdf Curriculum vitae of Jonah Rockoff from the website of Columbia University. Retrieved April 18th, 2018.]</ref>

== Research==

Jonah Rockoff's research focuses on teachers, in particular the measurement of teachers' impact, and public schools.

=== Research on teachers===

Revolving themes in Rockoff's research on teachers are teachers' effectiveness and the recruitment and retention of effective teachers. For example, he estimates that increasing teacher quality by one standard deviation raises reading and math test scores by approximately 0.20 and 0.24 standard deviations, respectively, and reading scores are found to increase in teachers' experience.<ref>{{cite journal | url=https://www.aeaweb.org/articles?id=10.1257/0002828041302244 | doi=10.1257/0002828041302244 | title=The Impact of Individual Teachers on Student Achievement: Evidence from Panel Data | date=2004 | last1=Rockoff | first1=Jonah E. | journal=American Economic Review | volume=94 | issue=2 | pages=247–252 }}</ref> Similarly, together with [[Donald Boyd|Boyd]], [[Susanna Loeb|Loeb]], [[James Wyckoff|Wyckoff]] and [[Hamilton Lankford|Lankford]], Rockoff finds that the NYC Teaching Fellows and Teach for America initiatives were able to substantially reduce the gap between the average qualifications of teachers at New York City's low- and high-poverty schools by substituting uncertified teachers in high-poverty schools by academically qualified teachers.<ref>{{cite journal | url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/pdf/10.1002/pam.20377 | doi=10.1002/pam.20377 | title=The narrowing gap in New York City teacher qualifications and its implications for student achievement in high-poverty schools | date=2008 | last1=Boyd | first1=Donald | last2=Lankford | first2=Hamilton | last3=Loeb | first3=Susanna | last4=Rockoff | first4=Jonah | last5=Wyckoff | first5=James | journal=Journal of Policy Analysis and Management | volume=27 | issue=4 | pages=793–818 | url-access=subscription }}</ref> Together with [[Thomas Kane (economist)|Kane]] and [[Douglas Staiger|Staiger]], Rockoff has also studied how to recruit effective teachers. In particular, they find that—unlike teachers' performance in classrooms during the first two years—teacher certification poorly predicts teachers' future effectiveness,<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Kane |first1=Thomas J. |last2=Rockoff |first2=Jonah E. |last3=Staiger |first3=Douglas O. |date=2008 |title=What does certification tell us about teacher effectiveness? Evidence from New York City |url=https://ideas.repec.org/a/eee/ecoedu/v27y2008i6p615-631.html |volume=27 |issue=6 |pages=615–631 |journal=Economics of Education Review|doi=10.1016/j.econedurev.2007.05.005 }}</ref> and that composite measures of teachers' cognitive and non-cognitive skills predict well teachers' effectiveness, though individual measures do not (with [[Brian Jacob]]).<ref>{{cite journal | url=https://www.mitpressjournals.org/doi/abs/10.1162/EDFP_a_00022 | doi=10.1162/EDFP_a_00022 | title=Can You Recognize an Effective Teacher when You Recruit One? | date=2011 | last1=Rockoff | first1=Jonah E. | last2=Jacob | first2=Brian A. | last3=Kane | first3=Thomas J. | last4=Staiger | first4=Douglas O. | journal=Education Finance and Policy | volume=6 | pages=43–74 }}</ref> This research is complemented by work with [[Cecilia Speroni]], wherein he principals' subjective evaluations of teacher effectiveness to be good predictors of teachers' objective effectiveness.<ref>{{cite journal | url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0927537111000315 | doi=10.1016/j.labeco.2011.02.004 | title=Subjective and objective evaluations of teacher effectiveness: Evidence from New York City | date=2011 | last1=Rockoff | first1=Jonah E. | last2=Speroni | first2=Cecilia | journal=Labour Economics | volume=18 | issue=5 | pages=687–696 | url-access=subscription }}</ref> Next to teacher recruitment, Rockoff's research on teacher retention has found that mentoring among [[New York City|NYC]] teachers has only a weak effect on teachers' turnover, absenteeism and their students' achievements, though there is some evidence that mentoring successfully improves school-specific knowledge and teaching skills.<ref>{{cite journal |last=Rockoff |first=J. E. |date=March 2008 |title=Does Mentoring Reduce Turnover and Improve Skills of New Employees? Evidence from Teachers in New York City |journal=NBER Working Paper No. 13868 |doi=10.3386/w13868 |doi-access=free }}</ref> Overall, Rockoff's views on the recruitment, evaluation and retention of teachers in the U.S. are summarized in a ''[[Journal of Economic Perspectives|JEP]]'' article with Staiger, wherein they highlight five empirical findings: (1) teachers' impact on student achievement vary strongly; (2) measures of teacher effectiveness based on student test scores are unreliable; (3) teachers' effectiveness increases degressively over their careers; (4) the main issue related to teacher turnover is the loss in student achievement as students are taught by less experienced teachers rather than the direct cost of hiring and firing teachers; and (5) identifying effective teachers at the time of recruitment—as opposed to based on their actual job performance—is very difficult.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Staiger |first1=Douglas O. |last2=Rockoff |first2=Jonah E. |date=2010 |title=Searching for Effective Teachers with Imperfect Information |url=https://www.aeaweb.org/articles?id=10.1257/jep.24.3.97 |journal=Journal of Economic Perspectives |language=en |volume=24 |issue=3 |pages=97–118 |doi=10.1257/jep.24.3.97  |doi-access=free|issn=0895-3309}}</ref>

More recently, Rockoff and his co-researchers [[Raj Chetty]] and [[John Friedman]] have researched better ways to measure the effectiveness of teachers, with a focus on the [[value-added modeling|value added methodology]]. They find that value-added provides unbiased estimates of teachers' impacts on student achievement and that teachers with high value-added improve students' adulthood outcomes, with a one standard deviation increase in a teacher's value-added in a single grade corresponding to a 1% increase in earnings of her students' earnings by age 28.<ref>[https://www0.gsb.columbia.edu/faculty/jrockoff/papers/value_added_dec2011.pdf Chetty, R., Friedman, J.N., Rockoff, J.E. (2011). The long-term impacts of teachers: Teacher value-added and student outcomes in adulthood. ''National Bureau of Economic Research'', No. 17699.]</ref><ref>{{cite journal | url=https://www.aeaweb.org/articles?id=10.1257/aer.104.9.2593 | doi=10.1257/aer.104.9.2593 | title=Measuring the Impacts of Teachers I: Evaluating Bias in Teacher Value-Added Estimates | date=2014 | last1=Chetty | first1=Raj | last2=Friedman | first2=John N. | last3=Rockoff | first3=Jonah E. | journal=American Economic Review | volume=104 | issue=9 | pages=2593–2632 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | url=https://www.aeaweb.org/articles?id=10.1257/aer.104.9.2633 | doi=10.1257/aer.104.9.2633 | title=Measuring the Impacts of Teachers II: Teacher Value-Added and Student Outcomes in Adulthood | date=2014 | last1=Chetty | first1=Raj | last2=Friedman | first2=John N. | last3=Rockoff | first3=Jonah E. | journal=American Economic Review | volume=104 | issue=9 | pages=2633–2679 }}</ref>

=== Research on public schools, crime and housing===

Next to his research on teachers, Rockoff has also studied public schools, the impact of sex offender registration, and the relationship between crime risk and property values. Together with Damon Clark and Paco Martorell, Rockoff finds scant evidence for a relationship between school performance and principals' education and work experience, though there is a benefit to having worked as assistant principal beforehand and students' test scores improve as principal experience increases, suggesting principal retention as an important policy issue.<ref>{{cite journal |url=https://files.eric.ed.gov/fulltext/ED509693.pdf |last1=Clark |first1=D. |last2=Martorell |first2=P. |last3=Rockoff |first3=J. |year=2009 |title=School Principals and School Performance |journal=Center for Analysis of Longitudinal Data in Education Research Working Paper Series No. 38 }}</ref> In his research with [[Caroline Hoxby]] on the impact of [[charter school]]s on student achievement, Rockoff finds that charter school attendance improves elementary school students' scores in math and reading by ca. six percentile point ranks.<ref>{{cite journal |url=https://www.rand.org/content/dam/rand/www/external/labor/seminars/adp/pdfs/2005hoxby.pdf |last1=Hoxby |first1=C. M. |last2=Rockoff |first2=J. E. |year=2004 |title=The impact of charter schools on student achievement |journal=Unpublished Working Paper |accessdate=April 13, 2018 }}</ref> In work with [[Leigh Linden]], Rockoff observes that house prices within 0.1  miles of a sex offender fall on average by 4%.<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1257/aer.98.3.1103 |last1=Linden |first1=L. |last2=Rockoff |first2=J. E. |year=2008 |title=Estimates of the impact of crime risk on property values from Megan's Laws |journal=[[American Economic Review]] |volume=98 |issue=3 |pages=1103–1127 |url=http://www.aeaweb.org/aer/data/june08/20060620_app.pdf }}</ref> Finally, in research with J.J. Prescott, Rockoff finds that both [[sex offender registration]] and notification reduce the frequency of reported sex offenses against local victims, though the former acts by keeping police informed about local sex offenders, whereas the latter deters non-registered offenders and actually may exacerbate  [[recidivism]].<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1086/658485 |last1=Prescott |first1=J. J. |last2=Rockoff |first2=J. E. |year=2011 |title=Do Sex Offender Registration and Notification Laws Affect Criminal Behaviour? |journal=Journal of Law and Economics |volume=54 |issue=1 |pages=161–206 |s2cid=1672265 |url=https://repository.law.umich.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=1079&context=articles }}</ref>
